title: AMERICANS Act
stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Armed Services.
plain_summary: This bill protects military members who were kicked out or punished for not getting COVID-19 vaccines. It makes sure these service members get honorable discharges and allows them to come back to the military with full back pay. The bill also stops the military from treating unvaccinated members unfairly and says they must get the same chances for jobs and promotions as everyone else. Finally, it prevents the military from requiring COVID-19 vaccines in the future unless Congress specifically says it is okay.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Armed Services.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Allowing Military Exemptions, Recognizing Individual Concerns About New Shots Act of two thousand twenty five or the AMERICANS Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Remedies for Members of the Armed Forces Discharged or Subject to Adverse Action Under the COVID-19 Vaccine Mandate
sections.text:
The Secretary of Defense cannot create any new COVID-nineteen vaccine requirement to replace the one that was canceled under a previous law. The Secretary can only create a new vaccine requirement if Congress passes another law that clearly allows it.

This section changes an existing law about military members and COVID-nineteen vaccines. The changes say that any military member who is kicked out because they refused the COVID-nineteen vaccine must receive an honorable discharge (a good type of discharge record).

The Secretary of Defense cannot punish a military member just because they refused to get the COVID-nineteen vaccine.

Military members who were kicked out or punished because of their COVID-nineteen vaccination status can ask for help. They can apply through a process the Secretary of Defense will create. The Secretary must do the following things for these members:

If a member was kicked out only because they refused the COVID-nineteen vaccine, and they would have gotten an honorable discharge otherwise, the Secretary must change their discharge status to honorable.

If a member wants to return to service, the Secretary must bring them back at the highest rank they held before being kicked out. However, if they lost rank for other reasons not related to vaccines, that rank reduction stays. Their return to service date will be counted as the same date they were originally kicked out.

If a member faced punishment other than being kicked out, the Secretary must give them back their highest rank from before the punishment, except for any rank loss not related to vaccines. The member must also be paid back for any lost pay and benefits.

The Secretary must remove from the member's service record any punishment or discharge related only to COVID-nineteen vaccination status.

For members who return to service, the time they were separated must count toward their retirement pay calculations.

The Secretary of Defense must try hard to keep military members who are not vaccinated against COVID-nineteen. These members must get equal opportunities for professional development, promotions, leadership roles, and consideration compared to other members.

The Secretary can only consider a member's COVID-nineteen vaccination status when making decisions about deployments, assignments, and operations if a foreign country's laws require vaccination to enter that country and the member must be in that country to do their job.

For the situations described above, the Secretary must create a process to give COVID-nineteen vaccination exemptions to members who have natural immunity to COVID-nineteen, have health conditions that make vaccination more risky for them than for most people, or have sincere religious beliefs that conflict with getting the COVID-nineteen vaccination.

Any former military member who was separated because they refused the COVID-nineteen vaccine does not have to pay back any bonuses they received. If they already paid back any portion of such bonuses before this law was passed, they must be reimbursed for those payments.

All the protections and remedies in this section apply to military members whether or not they asked for an accommodation to any Department of Defense COVID-nineteen vaccination policy for any reason.
